questions.question: What does calling `scope.stop()` do to an `effectScope`?
options: It pauses all effects until `scope.run()` is called again
It disposes all watchers, computed properties, and watchEffect calls created inside the scope at once
It prevents new effects from being added to the scope
It flushes all pending effect callbacks synchronously

questions.question: What is `onScopeDispose` analogous to in component lifecycle terms?
options: `onBeforeMount` — runs before the scope starts executing
`onUnmounted` — registers a cleanup callback that runs when the scope stops
`onUpdated` — runs after each time the scope's effects re-run
`onErrorCaptured` — catches errors thrown inside the scope

questions.question: When is `effectScope` most useful compared to just using individual `watch` stop handles?
options: When you need to share reactive state between sibling components
When a composable creates many effects and you want a single `cleanup()` call instead of tracking each stop handle
When you want effects to re-run on every component render
When you need watchers to fire synchronously without batching
